informal: Suppose that $E$ is an uncountable subset of $\mathbb{R}$. Prove that there exists a point $p \in \mathbb{R}$ at which $E$ condenses.
lean:
theorem Pugh_exercise_2_126 {E : Set ℝ}
  (hE : ¬ Set.Countable E) : ∃ (p : ℝ), ClusterPt p (𝓟 E)
reasoning: The informal statement asks for a *condensation point* of E: a point p such that every neighborhood of p contains uncountably many points of E. The Lean signature uses `ClusterPt p (𝓟 E)`, which only says p is a cluster (accumulation) point — every neighborhood of p meets E in a point other than p, i.e. contains at least one (hence infinitely many) points of E. This is strictly weaker than condensing: a cluster point need not have uncountably many points of E in every neighborhood. Since any uncountable subset of ℝ has a condensation point, the informal claim implies the Lean claim, but not conversely. The correct Mathlib notion would be something like requiring `¬ Set.Countable (E ∩ U)` for every neighborhood U of p (or `AccPt`/condensation point predicates), not `ClusterPt`.
